serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (250 g) is the typical single-serve portion for this hot beverage as sold at Singaporean food stalls and hot pot restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ginger tea, sweetened (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with brown sugar nutrient profile for sugar fraction
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for brown sugar ginger tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central sweetened ginger tea entries and cross-checked against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7). The input values (energy 40 kcal, protein 0.2 g, fat 0.04 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g, sugar 9.2 g, sodium 2 mg, dietary fibre 0.2 g per 100 g) are consistent with a lightly sweetened ginger infusion and are retained as the best available estimate. Cholesterol 0 mg and saturated fat 0 g are confirmed zero for this plant-based sugar-water beverage.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for sucrose/brown sugar solution
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brown sugar ginger tea. GI estimated at 65 based on: (1) brown sugar GI of approximately 64–65 per University of Sydney GI Database (sucrose-equivalent solution); (2) the beverage is predominantly a sugar-water solution (9.2 g sugar per 100 g, 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g); (3) ginger has a negligible independent effect on the GI of the beverage at typical concentrations. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 25.0) / 100 = 16.25, rounded to 16.3. Serving carbohydrate = 10.0 g/100 g × 250 g = 25.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.2 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage exceeds the AHA 2018 threshold of 5 g/100 g for sugar-sweetened beverages; liquid sugar independently raises blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention regardless of the low sodium content (2 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this beverage in its standard sweetened form; replacing brown sugar with stevia or consuming unsweetened ginger tea preserves ginger's vasodilatory benefit without the sugar-driven blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.2 g sugar per 100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.2 g/100 g); liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes that are particularly harmful in type 2 diabetes management per ADA 2023 Standards of Care.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage as formulated; unsweetened ginger tea retains ginger's evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties (gingerols) without the glycaemic load, and is the recommended substitution per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk via dyslipidaemia, elevated triglycerides, and endothelial inflammation; however, sodium is very low (2 mg/100 g) and saturated fat is zero, limiting direct lipid-pathway risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate this beverage due to its sugar content (9.2 g/100 g); ginger's anti-platelet and anti-inflammatory properties are beneficial but are negated by the pro-inflammatory effect of high sugar intake, and unsweetened ginger tea is the preferred alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Brown sugar at 9.2 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators (AGEs, cytokines) that counteract ginger's anti-inflammatory gingerol content; net effect is pro-inflammatory for active RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should prepare this as unsweetened ginger tea to benefit from gingerols' anti-inflammatory properties without the sugar-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Brown Sugar Ginger Tea (黑糖姜母茶, Hēi táng jiāng mǔ chá) is a traditional Chinese hot beverage prepared by simmering fresh ginger root in water and sweetening with brown sugar (黑糖, unrefined cane sugar). It is widely consumed in Singapore and southern China, particularly as a warming accompaniment to hot pot meals or as a comforting cold-weather drink. The beverage has a deep amber colour, a spicy-sweet flavour profile from the combination of pungent gingerols and the molasses notes of brown sugar, and a thin, watery texture. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 40 kcal, 10 g carbohydrate (of which 9.2 g is sugar), 0.2 g protein, 0.04 g fat, 0.2 g dietary fibre, and 2 mg sodium. Cholesterol and saturated fat are zero, consistent with its plant-based composition. Clinically, the high sugar content (9.2 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage, making it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. Ginger itself contains bioactive gingerols and shogaols with documented anti-inflammatory, hypoglycaemic, and vasodilatory properties; however, these benefits are substantially offset by the brown sugar content at typical serving concentrations. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the brown sugar solution profile, with a glycemic load of approximately 16.3 per 250 g cup. Unsweetened ginger tea is the clinically preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers. The beverage is vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.

notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for a brown sugar ginger tea infusion are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; the fat content is extremely low (0.04 g/100 g) and the fatty acid breakdown is nutritionally negligible. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this traditional beverage preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2024. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero for this plant-based beverage with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage containing no animal-derived ingredients. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero consistent with the near-zero total fat content and plant-based composition. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as supporting menu_listing only and was not used as a nutrient evidence source; nutrient values were validated against USDA FoodData Central. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on brown sugar solution; direct GI measurement for this beverage has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2024.
